metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_commands/session_log.py` (and `src/rebar/_commands/idea.py`) as consumers of `create_core`/`_compute_alias` that will also produce v2 aliases after the cutover, and confirm this is the intended behavior.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_commands/session_log.py` calls `create_core` directly and is an unenumerated consumer: session_log ticket creation will also start producing v2 (adjective-adjective-animal) aliases after the wiring change, but the plan does not acknowledge this.
findings.location: Scope / Design — 'Wired into the create path only: `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` `_compute_alias`'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the plan that `compute_genesis_alias` uses a separate loader function and separate module-level cache globals (e.g., `_WORDS_CACHE_V2`, `_WARNED_MISSING_V2`) isolated from the v1 `_load()` / `_WORDS_CACHE` globals.
findings.finding: The plan places `compute_genesis_alias` in `src/rebar/_alias.py`, which already owns module-level globals `_WORDS_CACHE` (a `tuple[list[str], list[str]] | None`) and `_WARNED_MISSING` tightly coupled to the v1 wordlist loader. The plan provides no description of how the new function avoids colliding with or corrupting these globals when both generators are active in the same process.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/Design section: '`compute_genesis_alias` uses its own `_load_v2()` function and `_WORDS_CACHE_V2` / `_WARNED_MISSING_V2` module globals; it does not call or share state with `_load()` or `_WORDS_CACHE`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Describe explicitly how `compute_genesis_alias` loads `ticket-wordlist-v2.txt` without conflicting with the v1 module-level `_WORDS_CACHE`/`_WARNED_MISSING` globals — e.g. add parallel `_WORDS_CACHE_V2`/`_WARNED_MISSING_V2` module globals, or move the new function to a separate `_alias_genesis.py` module with its own loader.
findings.finding: The plan co-locates `compute_genesis_alias` in `src/rebar/_alias.py`, which has module-level globals `_WORDS_CACHE` and `_WARNED_MISSING` hard-wired to the v1 wordlist path. The new function must load `ticket-wordlist-v2.txt`, a different file with a different section marker (`# ANIMALS` vs `# NOUNS`). The plan does not describe how these two loaders coexist without one corrupting the other's module-level cache — a concrete mechanism gap that will cause either the wrong wordlist to be loaded silently or a cache miss that re-loads v1 for every new-ticket create.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_WORDS_CACHE_V2: tuple[list[str], list[str]] | None = None` and `_WARNED_MISSING_V2: bool = False` as dedicated module-level globals, with a `_load_v2()` that resolves `ticket-wordlist-v2.txt` and caches into those names. `compute_genesis_alias` calls `_load_v2()` exclusively.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md/design-doc entry capturing the decision to adopt adjective-adjective-animal for new tickets (rationale, wordlist source, legacy coexistence strategy), and audit any user-facing docs that describe the alias format for staleness.
findings.finding: No ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry is planned to record the decision to switch new-ticket alias format from adjective-noun-noun to adjective-adjective-animal, even though this is a lasting, user-visible contract change.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/decisions/NNN-alias-format-v2.md` (or an equivalent ADR) noting: (1) old format retained for legacy read-time backfill, (2) new format for all CREATE events going forward, (3) wordlist provenance and license. Also grep for any README / help-text / changelog references to the alias format pattern and update them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'path independence' in AC5 precisely (e.g., 'the wordlist loads correctly regardless of the process working directory' or 'the generator produces identical output when called from any module').
findings.finding: 'Path independence' in AC5 is undefined — an executor cannot determine what behavior to test without clarification on whether it means wordlist-file-path portability, import-path independence, or something else.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'path independence' with the specific behavior: e.g., 'wordlist resource loads correctly when the process cwd differs from the package root (i.e., uses importlib.resources or equivalent, not a relative open() call)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `docs/licenses/` is a new directory to be created as part of this work (it does not currently exist), and confirm the license file will be committed alongside the wordlist.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'License vendored to `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt`', but the `docs/licenses/` directory does not exist in the repository. The plan does not state whether this directory is to be created as part of this work or whether it is assumed to already exist.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit 'New files' list entry: 'Create `docs/licenses/` directory with `adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt` (directory does not currently exist).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate `coolname` (PyPI, MIT) as a prior-art alternative to wordlist-vendoring before committing to `ticket-wordlist-v2.txt`; document in the plan why it was accepted or rejected.
findings.finding: The plan commits to vendoring the `a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` npm package's wordlist without considering `coolname` (PyPI), a Python-native library that provides exactly adj-adj-animal name generation with a bundled MIT-licensed wordlist — eliminating the need for custom wordlist vendoring, custom parsing, and a new file format entirely.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence rationale in Scope/Design, e.g.: 'coolname was considered but rejected because [determinism contract / no runtime deps / specific vocabulary required / install footprint] — wordlist is vendored instead.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the actual section-delimiter format of the `a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` upstream wordlist before committing to `# ANIMALS` as the parser marker; if the upstream format differs, adjust the `_load_v2` parser accordingly and document the actual delimiter used.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the new `ticket-wordlist-v2.txt` uses a `# ANIMALS` section marker to delimit adjectives from animals, but the upstream source (`a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` npm package) is not present in the repo. The existing wordlist uses `# NOUNS`, and no evidence exists to confirm the upstream package actually uses `# ANIMALS` as its delimiter. If the real upstream format differs, the parser will silently treat all words as adjectives.
findings.suggested_fix: Download the upstream wordlist, inspect its actual structure, and update the plan (and the `_load_v2` parser) to match the real delimiter. If the upstream file has no delimiter, split it into two explicit files (adjectives.txt, animals.txt) rather than relying on an in-band marker.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete de-collision mechanism to the `compute_genesis_alias` design (e.g., when adj1_index == adj2_index, add a deterministic offset such as `(adj1_index + 1) % N`) so the 'guaranteed distinct' constraint is provably satisfied for all valid input IDs.
findings.finding: AC1 requires the two adjectives to be 'guaranteed distinct', but the plan provides no mechanism description. With a naïve approach (e.g. `adj1 = adjs[int(hex[0:4],16) % N]`, `adj2 = adjs[int(hex[4:8],16) % N]`), any ticket id where both 4-hex slices reduce to the same list index produces `adjective-adjective-animal` with adj1 == adj2 — violating the AC. The guarantee requires an explicit de-collision step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 'two adjectives differ, deterministic from the ticket id'
findings.suggested_fix: adj1_idx = int(hex[0:4],16) % N; adj2_idx = int(hex[4:8],16) % N; if adj2_idx == adj1_idx: adj2_idx = (adj2_idx + 1) % N

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/licenses/` directory creation to the edit set (it does not exist yet), or note that the directory must be created as part of the license vendoring step.
findings.finding: `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt` is named as an edit target, but the parent directory `docs/licenses/` does not exist in the repository — the plan does not mention creating it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `docs/licenses/` to the Scope / Design file list: 'Create `docs/licenses/` directory and vendor license to `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit test cases to AC5 (and the test plan) for: (a) id shorter than 12 hex chars returns `None`, and (b) generated alias always has two distinct adjectives (e.g. assert first and second words differ across a sample of deterministic outputs).
findings.finding: AC5's test enumeration omits explicit coverage for the 'too-short (<12 hex) id → None' boundary and the 'two adjectives are guaranteed distinct' invariant, both of which are stated behavioral requirements in AC3 and the Scope/Design section.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 to read: 'Tests cover the new format, path independence, the missing-wordlist fallback, short-id (<12 hex) → None, and the distinct-adjectives invariant.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the effort-framed AC5 with a concrete proving command (e.g., `pytest src/rebar/tests/test_alias.py -v`) and name the test module/file so done-ness is verifiable.
findings.finding: AC5 uses 'tests cover' (effort-framed) rather than naming a concrete proving command (e.g., a pytest invocation or specific test module path), so there is no verifiable done-signal for the test criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: AC5: `pytest src/rebar/tests/test_alias.py` passes, covering: (a) new adjective-adjective-animal format with distinct adjectives, (b) determinism (same id → same alias), (c) too-short id returns None, (d) missing wordlist emits exactly one WARN and returns a hex alias.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief testing strategy to the plan body describing which test cases cover the new format, what 'path independence' means operationally, and how the missing-wordlist fallback will be exercised (e.g. tmp_path wordlist swap or importlib mock).
findings.finding: AC5 specifies a test deliverable ('Tests cover the new format, path independence, and the missing-wordlist fallback') but the plan body contains no test section or description of the testing approach, making it impossible to verify that the described test scope is achievable with the stated design.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5 vs. Scope/Design section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `compute_genesis_alias` needs to be a public named function (3+ call-sites) or should be private (`_compute_genesis_alias`) given it currently has only one production call-site (`composer._compute_alias`); if public, enumerate the additional consumers the plan anticipates.
findings.finding: `compute_genesis_alias` is a new named public function with exactly one identified production call-site (`composer.py` `_compute_alias`), which is below the Rule-of-Three threshold for introducing a standalone abstraction. The plan does not enumerate additional consumers (e.g., a future MCP write path, the delete path, or the reducer backfill) that would justify promoting it to a peer of `compute_alias`.
findings.location: Scope/Design — 'New generator `compute_genesis_alias` in `src/rebar/_alias.py`'; wired into `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` `_compute_alias` only
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to `_compute_genesis_alias` (private) and note in the plan that it is exposed for testing via the module's own test suite, keeping the public API surface minimal until a second production consumer exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite the upstream repository URL and confirm the SPDX license identifier (e.g. MIT) before merging; include the actual license text in `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt` and reference it in the plan/AC4.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` npm package and gfycat wordlists carry an MIT license, but no upstream URL, license text excerpt, or verification step is cited anywhere in the plan. The `docs/licenses/` directory does not yet exist in the repo. AC4 treats this as a done criterion ('Wordlist sourced from OSS with license vendored') without specifying how the license will be confirmed, creating an unverified external compliance precondition.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Source: https://github.com/a-type/adjective-adjective-animal (MIT, SPDX: MIT); gfycat word data originally from https://github.com/jipumarino/gfycat-style (MIT). License text copied verbatim to docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt.' This makes the compliance precondition verifiable by reviewers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description of the short-id guard (< 12 hex chars → None) and missing-wordlist fallback (hex alias + one-shot WARN) to the Scope/Design section so AC3 maps to explicit design choices.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies two behaviors — 'too-short (<12 hex) id → None' and 'missing wordlist falls back to a hex alias with a one-shot WARN' — that have no corresponding description in the Scope/Design section, leaving implementation details underspecified and untethered from the design narrative.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3 vs. Scope/Design section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under 'New generator compute_genesis_alias' in Scope/Design: 'Returns None for ids shorter than 12 hex chars. If the wordlist file is absent at import time, falls back to returning a hex-prefixed alias and emits a single WARN log (one-shot via logging.warn with stacklevel guard).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan describing how tickets created with the new alias format would be handled if the change is reverted (e.g. alias field can be regenerated on demand, or a migration script re-derives from legacy compute_alias).
findings.finding: The plan describes alias persistence as 'locking in the format' on the CREATE event — an irreversible per-ticket write — but provides no rollback path or back-out plan if the new generator produces undesirable aliases at scale (e.g. offensive word combinations) and deployed tickets need re-aliasing.
findings.location: Scope/Design: 'New tickets persist the alias onto the CREATE event, locking in the format'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concurrency-safe implementation of the one-shot WARN flag (e.g., `threading.Event`, module-level `logging.warn` with `stacklevel` + `warnings.warn(..., stacklevel=...) once=True`, or Python `warnings` module's built-in deduplication) and document its ownership and lifecycle in the design.
findings.finding: The 'one-shot WARN' for a missing wordlist implies a module-level (or singleton) mutable flag to track whether the warning has already been emitted. The plan does not define ownership, initialization, or concurrency safety for this flag. Under concurrent request handling (e.g., multiple threads hitting the create path simultaneously when the wordlist is absent), multiple threads can race on the flag check-then-set, potentially emitting duplicate warnings or, more importantly, leaving the flag unset in a way that masks repeated failures.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, line 3: 'missing wordlist falls back to a hex alias with a one-shot WARN'
findings.suggested_fix: Use Python's `warnings.warn(..., category=RuntimeWarning)` with the standard `warnings` filter (`'once'` or `'default'`), which provides built-in deduplication without introducing a custom mutable flag. This is thread-safe and requires no lifecycle management.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the 'What' or 'Scope/Design' section explaining *why* the adjective-adjective-animal format is preferable for new tickets (e.g., namespace size, memorability, OSS alignment).
findings.finding: The plan states what the format change is and how it will be implemented, but provides no positive rationale for *why* the alias format is being switched from adjective-noun-noun to adjective-adjective-animal (e.g., larger namespace, better memorability, user feedback, alignment with an external standard). G6 requires the plan to state a positive rationale for the chosen approach.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Scope/Design whether `ticket-wordlist-v2.txt` is a verbatim upstream file or a repo-convention file assembled from upstream word arrays (adjectives.js + animals.js); if assembled, describe the build/vendoring step.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the upstream `a-type/adjective-adjective-animal` wordlist is structured as a single file with a `# ANIMALS` section marker, but the actual gfycat/a-type source distributes adjectives and animals as *separate files* (adjectives.js / animals.js), not as a combined file with a section separator — meaning the claimed file format may be an author-invented convention, not a sourced upstream format.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The v2 wordlist is assembled from the upstream adjectives and animals arrays into the repo's existing `# SECTION` convention; it is not a verbatim upstream file.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
